title: Network Delay Time
problem_statement: You are given a network of n nodes, labeled from 1 to n. You are also given times, a list of travel times as directed edges times[i] = (ui, vi, wi), where ui is the source node, vi is the target node, and wi is the time it takes for a signal to travel from source to target. We will send a signal from a given node k. Return the time it takes for all the n nodes to receive the signal. If it is impossible for all the n nodes to receive the signal, return -1.

examples.explanation: At time 0, we send the signal from node 2. At time 1, node 1 and node 3 receive the signal. At time 2, node 4 receives the signal.

constraints: All the pairs (ui, vi) are unique. (i.e., no multiple edges.)
intuition: This is a shortest path problem from node k to all nodes. The answer is the maximum shortest path distance, or -1 if some node is unreachable.

approaches.name: Dijkstra's Algorithm
approaches.description: Use a priority queue to always process the node with the smallest current distance. Update distances to neighbors and track the max distance.
approaches.why_not: Bellman-Ford is O(V*E) and slower; Dijkstra is efficient for non-negative weights.
interview_commentary: Dijkstra finds shortest paths from k to all nodes. We use a PQ to always expand the closest node.
After running, check if all nodes have finite distance; if not, return -1. Else, return the max distance.
This assumes non-negative weights, which is given.

approaches.name: Bellman-Ford Algorithm
approaches.description: Relax all edges V-1 times. Since n<=100, it's feasible. Track distances and check for unreachable nodes.
approaches.why_not: Slower than Dijkstra for this case, but handles negative weights if needed.
interview_commentary: Bellman-Ford relaxes all edges in each of V-1 iterations. Here, since weights are positive, it's overkill but works.
We don't need the negative cycle check since the problem guarantees no negatives.
Same logic: find shortest paths and take max.

interview_qa.a: Because the signal reaches all nodes at different times; we need the time when the last node receives it.

interview_qa.q: What if the graph is disconnected?
interview_qa.a: If some node has infinite distance, return -1.
Dijkstra is faster for non-negative weights; Bellman-Ford is more general.
Only if all weights are 1; here weights vary, so Dijkstra or Bellman-Ford.
